q: The displacement and the increase in the velocity of a moving particle in the time interval of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>125</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, respectively. The distance travelled by the particle in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><msup><mo>)</mo><mtext>th </mtext></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is ___________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>.
question: The displacement and the increase in the velocity of a moving particle in the time interval of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>125</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, respectively. The distance travelled by the particle in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><msup><mo>)</mo><mtext>th </mtext></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is ___________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>.
solution:
Considering acceleration is constant, we can write

v=u+at

As increase in velocity in 1 s is given as 50 m s-1, therefore

⇒a=50 m s-2

Now, displacement covered between given time interval is, 

125=ut+12at2

⇒125=u+a2

⇒u=100 m s-1

Velocity after 1 s will be, v=100+50=150 m s-1

Therefore, displacement covered in t+1 s to t+2 s will 

∴Snth=vt'+12at'2, where t'=1 s

=175 m

The ratio of time of ascent to the time of descent will be equal to : [Use <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>].

The air resistance produces a constant retarding force of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> throughout the motion. The ratio of time of ascent to the time of descent will be equal to : [Use <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>].
solution:
Net force while going upward will be mg+R=50+10=60 N

⇒aup=605=12 m s-2

Similarly, Net force while going downward will be mg-R=40 N

⇒ad=405=8 m s-2

Since 

h=12at2⇒auptup2adtd2=1⇒tuptd=812=23

If the box was initially at rest, then _______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> is displacement along the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>

F→=20i^+10j^

a→=F→m=20i^+10j^2⇒10i^+5j^

∴ s→=12a→t2=1210i^+5j^×102

⇒5010i^+5j^m

∴ Displacement along x-axis

⇒50×10⇒500 m

q: An automobile travelling with speed of $60 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$, can brake to stop within a distance of $20 \mathrm{~cm}$. If the car is going twice as fast, i.e $120 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$, the stopping distance will be
question: An automobile travelling with speed of $60 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$, can brake to stop within a distance of $20 \mathrm{~cm}$. If the car is going twice as fast, i.e $120 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$, the stopping distance will be
solution: If the initial speed is doubled, the stopping distance becomes four times, i.e. $80 \mathrm{~m}$.

q: A ball is thrown vertically upwards with a velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>19</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> from the top of a tower. The ball strikes the ground after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. The height from the ground up to which the ball can rise will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mfrac><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mn>5</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi></math> is _____ (use <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>9</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: A ball is thrown vertically upwards with a velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>19</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> from the top of a tower. The ball strikes the ground after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. The height from the ground up to which the ball can rise will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mfrac><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mn>5</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi></math> is _____ (use <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>9</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
Initial velocity of ball is u=19.6 m s-1, final velocity v=0.

Using v=u+at, here, acceleration a=-g.

Time taken in upward motion above tower is ta=ug=19.69.8=2 s

Now, time taken from top most point to ground is td=6-2=4 s.

Or, td=2hmaxg    (Using s=ut+12gt2)

⇒hmax=16×9.82=3925 m.

Hence, the value of k=392.

q: A car starting from rest accelerates at the rate $f$ through a distance $S$, then continues at constant speed for time $t$ and then decelerates at the rate $f / 2$ to come to rest. If the total distance traversed is $15 \mathrm{~S}$, then
question: A car starting from rest accelerates at the rate $f$ through a distance $S$, then continues at constant speed for time $t$ and then decelerates at the rate $f / 2$ to come to rest. If the total distance traversed is $15 \mathrm{~S}$, then
solution: $$ \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{S}=\frac{\mathrm{ft}_1^2}{2} \\ & \mathrm{v}_0=\sqrt{2 \mathrm{Sf}} \end{aligned} $$ During retardation $$ S_2=2 S $$ During constant velocity $$ \begin{aligned} & 15 \mathrm{~S}-3 \mathrm{~S}=12 \mathrm{~S}=\mathrm{v}_0 \mathrm{t} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{S}=\frac{\mathrm{ft}^2}{72} \end{aligned} $$

question:
An object of mass m is being moved with a constant velocity under the action of an applied force of 2 N along a frictionless surface with following surface profile.

The correct applied force vs distance graph will be :
solution:
When the block will be moving up the incline, the external force will be up the incline and will be balancing the down the incline component of gravity.

When the block will be moving down the incline, the external force will be again up the incline and will be balancing the down the incline component of gravity. But in this case the external force will be on the back side of the block.

F=mgsinθ=2 N

subject: Physics
q:
Water drops fall from a tap on the floor, 5 m below, at regular intervals of time, the first drop strikes the floor when the sixth drop begins to fall. The height at which the fourth drop will be from ground, at the instant when the first drop strikes the ground is $\_\_\_\_$ m.
$\left(\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}\right)$
question:
Water drops fall from a tap on the floor, 5 m below, at regular intervals of time, the first drop strikes the floor when the sixth drop begins to fall. The height at which the fourth drop will be from ground, at the instant when the first drop strikes the ground is $\_\_\_\_$ m.
$\left(\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}\right)$
solution:
Time for first drop to fall 5 m:
$5 = \frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times t^2$ gives $t = 1$ s.
Since the first drop strikes when the sixth begins, there are 5 intervals: each interval $T = \frac{1}{5} = 0.2$ s.
When drop 1 hits the ground (at $t = 1$ s), drop 4 has been falling for $t_4 = 1 - 3 \times 0.2 = 0.4$ s.
Distance fallen by drop 4: $s_4 = \frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times (0.4)^2 = 0.8$ m.
Height of drop 4 above ground: $5 - 0.8 = 4.2$ m.

solution:
gt-1222×12=81 ... (1)
12gt2=100 …. (2)
devide equation 1 & 2
tt-12=109

t=5 s

g=8 ms2

subject: Physics
q:
A particle starts moving from time $t=0$ and its coordinate is given as $x(t)=4 t^{3}-3 t$
A. The particle returns to its original position (origin) 0.866 units later
B. The particle is 1 unit away from origin at its turning point
C. Acceleration of the particle is non-negative
D. The particle is 0.5 units away from origin at its turning point
E. Particle never turns back as acceleration is non-negative
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
A particle starts moving from time $t=0$ and its coordinate is given as $x(t)=4 t^{3}-3 t$
A. The particle returns to its original position (origin) 0.866 units later
B. The particle is 1 unit away from origin at its turning point
C. Acceleration of the particle is non-negative
D. The particle is 0.5 units away from origin at its turning point
E. Particle never turns back as acceleration is non-negative
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
Given $x(t) = 4t^3 - 3t$, we find $v(t) = 12t^2 - 3$ and $a(t) = 24t$.
Statement A: Setting $x(t) = 0$ gives $4t^3 - 3t = 0 \Rightarrow t(4t^2-3) = 0$, so $t = 0$ or $t = \sqrt{3/4} = 0.866$. The particle returns to origin at $t = 0.866$ units. TRUE.
Statement B: Turning points occur at $v(t) = 0 \Rightarrow 12t^2 - 3 = 0 \Rightarrow t = \pm 0.5$. At $t = 0.5$: $x = 4(0.125) - 1.5 = -1$; distance = 1 unit. At $t = -0.5$: $x = 1$; distance = 1 unit. TRUE.
Statement C: For $t \geq 0$ (particle starts at $t=0$), $a(t) = 24t \geq 0$. TRUE.
Statement D: At turning points, distance is 1 unit, not 0.5. FALSE.
Statement E: False because acceleration is non-negative only for $t \geq 0$, and particle does turn back at $t = 0.5$. FALSE.
Correct statements: A, B, C Only.

subject: Physics
q: A gas balloon is going up with a constant velocity of $10$ m/s. When this balloon reached a height of $75$ m, a stone is dropped from it and balloon keeps moving up with the same velocity. The height of the balloon when the stone hits the ground is ________ m. (Take $g=10$ m/s$^2$)
question: A gas balloon is going up with a constant velocity of $10$ m/s. When this balloon reached a height of $75$ m, a stone is dropped from it and balloon keeps moving up with the same velocity. The height of the balloon when the stone hits the ground is ________ m. (Take $g=10$ m/s$^2$)
solution:
When the stone is dropped, it acquires the initial velocity of the balloon. Taking the upward direction as positive, the initial velocity of the stone is $u = 10$ m/s.

The displacement of the stone when it hits the ground is $S = -75$ m, and its acceleration is $a = -g = -10$ m/s$^2$.

Using the equation of motion $S = ut + \dfrac{1}{2}at^2$:

$-75 = 10t - \dfrac{1}{2}(10)t^2$

$-75 = 10t - 5t^2$

$5t^2 - 10t - 75 = 0$

$t^2 - 2t - 15 = 0$

$(t - 5)(t + 3) = 0$

Since time cannot be negative, $t = 5$ s.

During this time, the balloon continues to move upwards with a constant velocity of $10$ m/s. The distance travelled by the balloon in $5$ s is:

$d = v \times t = 10 \times 5 = 50$ m

The total height of the balloon when the stone hits the ground is:

$H = 75 + 50 = 125$ m

Answer: $125$

⇒2vdvdx=2⇒a=22=1 m s-2.

subject: Physics
q: A particle of mass <math><mi>m</mi></math> and charge <math><mi>q</mi></math> is released from rest in a uniform electric field. If there is no other force on the particle, the dependence of its speed <math><mi>v</mi></math> on the distance <math><mi>x</mi></math> travelled by it is correctly given by (graphs are schematic and not drawn to scale)
question: A particle of mass <math><mi>m</mi></math> and charge <math><mi>q</mi></math> is released from rest in a uniform electric field. If there is no other force on the particle, the dependence of its speed <math><mi>v</mi></math> on the distance <math><mi>x</mi></math> travelled by it is correctly given by (graphs are schematic and not drawn to scale)
solution:
The particle is at rest initially.

So, the initial velocity u=0 m s-1.

The force on the charged particle in the uniform electric field,

F=qE

⇒ ma=qE

⇒ a=qEm

Applying the velocity-displacement equation under constant acceleration,

v2=u2+2ax

⇒ v2=02+2qEmx

⇒ v2=2qEmx

q: When a ball is dropped into a lake from a height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>9</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> above the water level, it hits the water with a velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi></math> and then sinks to the bottom with the constant velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi></math>. It reaches the bottom of the lake <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> after it is dropped. The approximate depth of the lake is
question: When a ball is dropped into a lake from a height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>9</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> above the water level, it hits the water with a velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi></math> and then sinks to the bottom with the constant velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi></math>. It reaches the bottom of the lake <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> after it is dropped. The approximate depth of the lake is
solution:
Using s=ut+12at2

Initially, u=0, so time taken to reach from A to B is  t=2sg=2×4.99.8=1 s

Now, using v2=u2+2as, we have

v=2gh=2×9.8×4.9=9.8 m s-1 

∵ t2=4-1 s=3 s  

∴  d=t2×v=3×9.8=29.4 m

subject: Physics
q: A bullet of mass <math><mi> </mi><mn>20</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> has an initial speed of <math><mi> </mi><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, just before it starts penetrating a mud wall of thickness <math><mi> </mi><mn>20</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. If the wall offers a mean resistance of <math><mi> </mi><mn>2.5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the speed of the bullet after emerging from the other side of the wall is close to:
question: A bullet of mass <math><mi> </mi><mn>20</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> has an initial speed of <math><mi> </mi><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, just before it starts penetrating a mud wall of thickness <math><mi> </mi><mn>20</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. If the wall offers a mean resistance of <math><mi> </mi><mn>2.5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the speed of the bullet after emerging from the other side of the wall is close to:
solution:
a=-2.5×10-220×10-3=-54 m s-2
From v2-u2=2as
v2=u2+2as
v=12-2×54×20×10-2
=0.5
=0.7 m s-1

subject: Physics
q: Water drops are falling from a nozzle of a shower onto the floor from a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The drops fall at a regular interval of time. When the first drop strikes the floor, at that instant, the third drop begins to fall. Locate the position of second drop from the floor when the first drop strikes the floor.
question: Water drops are falling from a nozzle of a shower onto the floor from a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The drops fall at a regular interval of time. When the first drop strikes the floor, at that instant, the third drop begins to fall. Locate the position of second drop from the floor when the first drop strikes the floor.
solution:
For first drop.

h=12g(2n)2

For 2nd  drop

h'=12g(n)2

hh'=41

h'=h4=9.84

so height of 2nd  drop

H=h-h'=9.8-9.84=34×9.8=7.35 m

subject: Physics
q:
Two spherical balls having equal masses with radius of 5 cm each are thrown upwards along the same vertical direction at an interval of 3 s with the same initial velocity of 35 m s-1, then these balls collide at a height of _____m,

(take g=10 m s-2)
question:
Two spherical balls having equal masses with radius of 5 cm each are thrown upwards along the same vertical direction at an interval of 3 s with the same initial velocity of 35 m s-1, then these balls collide at a height of _____m,

(take g=10 m s-2)
solution:
S1=S2

35t+12(−g)t2=35(t−3)+12(−g)(t−3)2

35t−12gt2=35t−35×3−12gt2−6t+9

35×3+45=30t

t=15030=5

height h=35×5−12×10×52

h=175−125=50 m

subject: Physics
q: A particle starts from the origin at time t=0 and moves along the positive x-axis. The graph of velocity with respect to time is shown in figure. What is the position of the particle at time t=5s?
question: A particle starts from the origin at time t=0 and moves along the positive x-axis. The graph of velocity with respect to time is shown in figure. What is the position of the particle at time t=5s?
solution:
Area enclosed by V-T graph=displacement (As particle moving in same direction)

12×2×2+2×2+1×3= Displacement

∴ So, displacement =9m

subject: Physics
q: An object moves with speed v1, v2 and v3 along a line segment AB, BC and CD respectively as shown in figure. Where AB = BC and AD = 3 AB, then average speed of the object will be :
question: An object moves with speed v1, v2 and v3 along a line segment AB, BC and CD respectively as shown in figure. Where AB = BC and AD = 3 AB, then average speed of the object will be :
solution:
Speed of an object is defined as the distance travelled by the object per unit time.

Let's assume that, AB= d. So, BC= d, AD= 3d. Also, assume that the time required by the object to travel AB, BC, CD are t1, t2, t3 respectively.

Now,

AD= 3ABAB+BC+CD= 3ABAB+AB+CD= 3ABCD= AB

Hence,

t1= ABv1= dv1t2= dv2t3= dv3

The formula to calculate the average speed va of the object is given by

va= ADt1+t2+t3........................(1)

Substitute the expressions for the parameters into equation (1) and simplify to obtain the required average speed of the object.

va= 3ddv1+dv2+dv3= 3v1v2v3v1v2+v2v3+v3v1

subject: Physics
q: The velocity time graphs of a car and a scooter are shown in the figure. (i) The difference between the distance travelled by the car and the scooter in 15 s and (ii) the time at which the car will catch up with the scooter are, respectively.
question: The velocity time graphs of a car and a scooter are shown in the figure. (i) The difference between the distance travelled by the car and the scooter in 15 s and (ii) the time at which the car will catch up with the scooter are, respectively.
solution:
Distance travelled by car in 15 s =12×45×15×=6752 m. Distance travelled by scooter in 15 s=30 ×15=450. Let car catches scooter in time t
difference between the distance travelled by car and scooter in 15 s in⇒ 450−6752=112.5  m
⇒6752+45t-15=30t
337.5+45t-675=30t  ⇒15 t=337.5 
⇒t=22.5 s.

subject: Physics
q: Water droplets are coming from an open tap at a particular rate. The spacing between a droplet observed at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>4</mn><mi>th</mi></msup></math> second after its fall to the next droplet is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>34</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. At what rate the droplets are coming from the tap ? (Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>9</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: Water droplets are coming from an open tap at a particular rate. The spacing between a droplet observed at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>4</mn><mi>th</mi></msup></math> second after its fall to the next droplet is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>34</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. At what rate the droplets are coming from the tap ? (Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>9</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
In 4th sec, 1st drop will travel 

⇒12×9.8×42=78.4 m

∴ 2nd drop would have travelled

⇒78.4-34.3=44.1 m

Time for 2nd drop

129.8t2=44.1

t=3 sec

∴ Each drop have time gap of 1 sec.

∴ 1 drop per second.

subject: Physics
q:
Match Column-I with Column-II :

  Column-I (x-t graphs)   Column-II (v-t graphs)
A I
B II
C III
D IV

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Match Column-I with Column-II :

  Column-I (x-t graphs)   Column-II (v-t graphs)
A I
B II
C III
D IV

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
(A) Here, the slope dxdt≥0 is always increasing, and we know that the derivative graph of the parabola is straight line, so the graph of velocity-time is straight line with increasing slope.

(A - II)

(B) Here, the slope dxdt<0 and at t→∞,dxdt→0. As the slope is decreasing with time, so the graph of velocity-time will have negative velocity and positive slope.

(B - IV)

(C) Here, the slope dxdt>0 for first half and dxdt<0 for second half.

As the slope of displacement-time is positive constant in first half and negative constant in second half, so the velocity will be uniform positive and uniform negative value.

(C - III)

(D) Here, the slope dxdt=constant.

As the slope of displacement-time is positive constant, so the velocity will be uniform positive value.

(D - I)

subject: Physics
q: A bullet is fired into a fixed target looses one third of its velocity after travelling <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. It penetrates further <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>D</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> before coming to rest. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>D</mi></math> is :
question: A bullet is fired into a fixed target looses one third of its velocity after travelling <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. It penetrates further <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>D</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> before coming to rest. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>D</mi></math> is :
solution:
When the bullet just enters the target, its equation of motion is given by

v2-u2=2aS   ...1

Equation (1) implies that

2u32=u2+2-a4×10-2⇒4u29=u2-2a4×10-2⇒5u29=2a4×10-2   …2

When the bullet stops inside the target, its equation of motion can be written as

0=2u32+2-ax   ...3

Equation (3) implies that

4u29=2ax   ...4

Dividing equation (2) by equation (4), we have

54=4×10-2x⇒x=165×10-2⇒x=3.2×10-2 m=32×10-3 m

Hence, D=32.

subject: Physics
q: A horse rider covers half the distance with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> speed. The remaining part of the distance was travelled with speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> for half the time and with speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> for other half of the time. The mean speed of the rider averaged over the whole time of motion is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>7</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ______.
question: A horse rider covers half the distance with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> speed. The remaining part of the distance was travelled with speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> for half the time and with speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> for other half of the time. The mean speed of the rider averaged over the whole time of motion is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>7</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ______.
solution:
Let total distance travelled by horse rider be 2x.

Now, the time taken to cover first half distance is tAB=x5

Second half distance x between BC is covered in two parts, x=d1+d2

here d1 is the distance travelled with 10  m s-1 and d2 is the distance travelled with 15 m s-1 in equal time intervals t2 each.

Then, d1=10t2, d2=15t2

Second half distance x=t2(10+15)=25t2

Now, the mean speed of rider is v=2xx5+2x25=2×255+2=507  m s-1

Thus, the value of x=50.

subject: Physics
q: A parachutist after bailing out falls $50 \mathrm{~m}$ without friction. When parachute opens, it decelerates at $2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$. He reaches the ground with a speed of $3 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. At what height, did he bail out?
question: A parachutist after bailing out falls $50 \mathrm{~m}$ without friction. When parachute opens, it decelerates at $2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$. He reaches the ground with a speed of $3 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. At what height, did he bail out?
solution:
$s=50+\left(\frac{3^2-(2 \times 10 \times 50)}{2(-2)}\right)$
$=293 \mathrm{~m}$.

subject: Physics
q: A train starting from rest first accelerates uniformly up to a speed of $80 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$ for time $t$, then it moves with a constant speed for time $3 t$. The average speed of the train for this duration of journey will be (in $\mathrm{km} / \mathrm{h}$ ) :
question: A train starting from rest first accelerates uniformly up to a speed of $80 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$ for time $t$, then it moves with a constant speed for time $3 t$. The average speed of the train for this duration of journey will be (in $\mathrm{km} / \mathrm{h}$ ) :
solution: $\begin{aligned} \text { Average speed } & =\frac{\text { total distance }}{\text { time taken }} \\ & =\frac{\frac{80 \times \mathrm{t}}{2}+80 \times 3 \mathrm{t}}{4 \mathrm{t}}=70 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{hr} .\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: The velocity-time graphs of a car and a scooter are shown in the figure. (i) the difference between the distance travelled by the car and the scooter in $15 \mathrm{~s}$ and (ii) the time at which the car will catch up with the scooter are, respectively
question: The velocity-time graphs of a car and a scooter are shown in the figure. (i) the difference between the distance travelled by the car and the scooter in $15 \mathrm{~s}$ and (ii) the time at which the car will catch up with the scooter are, respectively
solution:
Using equation, $a=\frac{v-u}{t}$ and
$$
S=u t+\frac{1}{2} a t^2
$$
Distance travelled by car in $15 \mathrm{sec}$
$=\frac{1}{2} \frac{(45)}{15}(15)^2$
$=\frac{675}{2} \mathrm{~m}$
Distance traveled by scooter in 15 seconds $=30 \times 15=450(\because$ distance $=$ speed $\times$ time $)$ Difference between distance travelled by car and scooter in $15 \mathrm{sec}, 450-337.5=112.5 \mathrm{~m}$ Let car catches scooter in time t;
$\frac{675}{2}+45(t-15)=30 t$
$337.5+45 \mathrm{t}-675=30 \mathrm{t}$
$\Rightarrow \quad 15 \mathrm{t}=337.5$
$\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{t}=22.5 \mathrm{sec}$

subject: Physics
q: A bullet is shot vertically downwards with an initial velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> from a certain height. Within <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>, the bullet reaches the ground and instantaneously comes to rest due to the perfectly inelastic collision. The velocity-time curve for total time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> will be : (Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: A bullet is shot vertically downwards with an initial velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> from a certain height. Within <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>, the bullet reaches the ground and instantaneously comes to rest due to the perfectly inelastic collision. The velocity-time curve for total time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> will be : (Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
As the upward direction is taken as positive, the velocity at t=0 is u=-100 m s-1.

As it is falling under gravity, its acceleration is constant and a=-10 m s-2.

Applying equation of motion for constant acceleration during the time of fall i.e., for 0 to 10 s,

v=u+at⇒v=-100-10×10⇒v=-200 m s-1

After 10 s it hits the ground and remains at rest.

Therefore, option A is the correct representation.

subject: Physics
q: A body falling under gravity covers two points A and B separated by 80 m in 2 s. The distance of upper point A from the starting point is _____m. Use g=10 m s-2
question: A body falling under gravity covers two points A and B separated by 80 m in 2 s. The distance of upper point A from the starting point is _____m. Use g=10 m s-2
solution:
From A→B, using the equation of motion, we have

-80=-v1t-12×10t2⇒-80=-2v1-12×10×22⇒-80=-2v1-20⇒-60=-2v1⇒v1=30 m s-1

From O to A, it follows that

v2=u2+2gS⇒302=0+2×(-10)(-S)⇒900=20 S⇒S=45 m

subject: Physics
q: An object is thrown vertically upwards. At its maximum height, which of the following quantity becomes zero ?
question: An object is thrown vertically upwards. At its maximum height, which of the following quantity becomes zero ?
solution:
When a ball is thrown vertically upwards, at the maximum height the velocity is zero.

As momentum is p=mv, so momentum is also zero at maximum height.

subject: Physics
q: The distance <math><mi>x</mi></math> covered by a paritcle in one dimensional motion varies with time <math><mi>t</mi></math> as <math><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mi>a</mi><msup><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>b</mi><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>c</mi></math> . If the acceleration of the particle depends on <math><mi>x</mi></math> as <math><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mi>n</mi></mrow></msup></math> , where <math><mi>n</mi></math> is an integer, the value of <math><mi>n</mi></math> is _________
question: The distance <math><mi>x</mi></math> covered by a paritcle in one dimensional motion varies with time <math><mi>t</mi></math> as <math><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mi>a</mi><msup><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>b</mi><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>c</mi></math> . If the acceleration of the particle depends on <math><mi>x</mi></math> as <math><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mi>n</mi></mrow></msup></math> , where <math><mi>n</mi></math> is an integer, the value of <math><mi>n</mi></math> is _________
solution:
x2=at2+2bt+c
2xv=2at+2b
xv=at+b
v2+ax=a
ax=a-at+bx2
a=aat2+2bt+c-at+b2x3
a=ac-2x3
a α x-3
